reasoning:
Response B is the stronger submission overall because it is substantially more complete and coherent as an end-to-end app, while Response A is visibly truncated/incomplete (e.g., Column/Card flow cuts off mid-implementation) and contains hard build/runtime issues in shown files (notably conflicting Search icon definitions/import usage and unfinished component logic). Architecturally, B provides a full Board/Column/Card/App flow with context + reducer + custom hooks, even though some concerns are imperfectly wired. A has good intent and richer requirement-aligned pieces in places (notably keyboard card movement hints and filter dimming intent), but the partial implementation prevents confidence in actual functionality.

TypeScript quality is better in B despite imperfections; A has strict config but includes likely compile problems (JSX in a .ts utility file and conflicting symbol definitions). For drag-and-drop, B delivers a full HTML5 DnD path across components/hooks; A appears to aim for stronger visual placeholder behavior but is incomplete, so B is more credible functionally. Accessibility/keyboard support is the one area where A aligns better with the prompt (Alt+Arrow, ARIA/live-region touches), while B explicitly leaves keyboard move logic as a TODO comment and lacks robust ARIA/live announcements. Search/filter/undo also favors A in intent and structure: B’s filter result is computed but not actually applied for dimming behavior, and undo wiring is largely non-functional (no-op undo button path in Board/SearchBar and incomplete history integration).

On project setup/code quality, both have notable issues, but B is still more runnable-looking as a complete project body; A’s truncation and unresolved code-level breaks are more severe. Therefore B wins overall.
